title: Kotlety Schabowe
subtitle: Polish breaded pork cutlets
excerpt: Pork loin cutlets hammered thin, breaded in the classic flour-egg-breadcrumb sequence, and fried in lard until the crust is shattering and golden. The Polish national dish.
sourceNotes: Kotlety schabowe are arguably Poland's national dish — the breaded pork cutlet that appears on every restaurant menu and in every household. Documented in Maria Lemnis and Henryk Vitry, Old Polish Traditions in the Kitchen and at the Table (1979). The technique is identical to Wiener Schnitzel and German Schweineschnitzel.

content.text: The quality of a kotlet schabowy depends on the pounding and the frying. The pork must be beaten to 5 to 6 mm thickness, so it cooks through by the time the breadcrumb crust reaches the right colour. Under-pounded pork will be raw inside before the crust darkens. The fat for frying should be lard: it has a higher smoke point than butter and gives the crust a particular savouriness.
The classic accompaniment is mashed potato and shredded sauerkraut or pickled cucumber salad. In Poland, a kotlet without potato is considered incomplete.

items.prepNote: cut into 4 slices across the grain, about 1.5 cm thick

content.text: Place each pork slice between two sheets of cling film or in a zip-lock bag. Pound with a rolling pin or meat mallet to an even thickness of about 5 to 6 mm. Season both sides with salt and pepper.

Set up three dishes: flour, beaten egg, and breadcrumbs. Coat each cutlet in flour (shaking off excess), then egg, then breadcrumbs, pressing the crumbs firmly on both sides. Melt the lard in a large frying pan over medium-high heat until hot. Fry the cutlets, 2 at a time if the pan allows, for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deep golden brown. Do not move them during frying until the crust sets. Drain on kitchen paper and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Crust is golden but pork is still raw
items.fix: Pound to 5 mm maximum; thicker pork needs lower heat and longer frying time

items.cause: Fat not hot enough; the coating steamed rather than fried
items.fix: Heat the fat properly before adding the cutlets — it should shimmer and a breadcrumb dropped in should sizzle immediately

items.symptom: Breading comes away in the pan
items.cause: Insufficient flour layer or egg not coating evenly
items.fix: Dust with flour on all sides thoroughly before the egg dip; the flour is what the egg adheres to

content.text: Kotlet schabowy arrived in Polish cooking in the 19th century, influenced by Viennese schnitzel via Austrian and Prussian food culture. It is now so thoroughly Polish that most Poles would be surprised to hear the connection. Every milk bar (bar mleczny) in Poland serves kotlet schabowy as the centrepiece of a two-złoty lunch; every restaurant has it on the menu; every Polish grandmother has her recipe.
